title: Name and Value
statement:
Empty editor. Build the listing shown in the target on the right.

Two parts, and on screen they look almost identical — lines listed one under another.

But in one of them every item stands on its own. In the other, every line is a PAIR: a name and a value.

One of the two is a list. The other isn't.
constraints: First part: What comes with it — Pump, Two keys, Bottle holder
Frame — Aluminium · Wheels — 26 inches · Gears — 21 · Weight — 12 kg
Not a single colon on the page

checks.t4: There is a list of pairs

why.empty: The editor is empty. There is no starter code here — you build the listing yourself.
why.not-closed:
Some tag is opened and never closed.

Four pairs means eight tags inside, and eight closing ones. And the list itself closes at the very end.
why.no-heading: There is no main heading, or it is empty.
why.no-sections: There aren't two subheadings. What comes with the bike and the specification are two separate parts.
why.no-dl:
The specification isn't a list of pairs.

Build it with a plain list and a colon and your code says: "here are four things of one kind". But they aren't four things. They're four pairs.

The colon is something only you understand.
why.no-dt:
There aren't four names.

In every pair the name comes first — frame, wheels, gears, weight. One for each line.
why.no-dd:
There aren't four values.

Every name wants an answer right after it. Asking without answering does no work.
why.empty-dt: There is an empty name. An empty name asks nothing.
why.empty-dd: There is an empty value. An empty value answers nothing.
why.no-ul:
There is no plain list.

The pump, the keys and the bottle holder aren't pairs — they're three things of one kind. None of them is the name of anything.
why.few-items: The plain list doesn't have three items.
why.empty-items: There is an empty item in the list.
why.kept-colon:
A colon is left over.

You needed it while you had nothing to separate a name from a value with. Now the tags do that.

A colon inside the name is old thinking with new tags. There it separates nothing — it just hangs.

no-dl: Look at the two parts and ask both the same thing: is this item the name of something else?
A pump isn't the name of anything — it's a pump. But "frame" without "aluminium" means nothing.
Lesson 28: there's a tag for exactly a list of pairs, and inside it the name and the value alternate.
no-dt: Every pair is two things: a question and an answer.
The name comes first — it's the term. Four lines, four names.
All four pairs live inside one shared list, not four separate ones.
kept-colon: Read the page on screen. Do you see a colon anywhere?
Look at the names. Is there anything in them besides the name itself?
The browser already sets the value apart on its own — look at the indent. The colon is left over from the previous way.

solution.why:
The first instinct is to build both parts with the same list and add a colon where separation is needed. On screen you get exactly what you wanted.

But look at what the code claims: "here are four things of one kind". The specification isn't four things. It's four pairs — a name and a value, each meaningful only together.

"Pump" means something on its own. "Aluminium" on its own means nothing — it's an answer to a question only the colon asks. And the colon is a sign only you can read.

The tags say it for you. And then the colon has no job left — it was standing in for something you didn't have.
steps: Start with the heading and the two subheadings.
The first part is an enumeration of three things. Each stands on its own — a plain list.
The second part isn't an enumeration. Go through the lines and ask: is this the name of something else?
Make it a list of pairs: the name first, the value right after it.
All four pairs go into one shared list, not four separate ones.
Finally, hunt for the colons. Each of them was standing in for something you now have.
solution.code:
Mountain bike

What comes with it

  • Pump
  • Two keys
  • Bottle holder

Specification

Frame
Aluminium
Wheels
26 inches
Gears
21
Weight
12 kg
solution.note:
On screen the two parts look almost the same — lines one under another.

The difference is in what isn't visible: Google reads a list of pairs and understands it's a specification. It can show it straight in the search results. From a list with colons it understands nothing.
